claims.summary: High-fat and greasy foods are commonly reported GERD triggers and may be reasonable to limit when they reproduce symptoms.
claims.applicability: Use when a fatty or fried preparation is a meaningful part of the meal, with more concern for a large or dominant exposure.
claims.limitations: The ACG guideline describes trigger-food avoidance as conditional and supported by low-quality evidence. Individual response matters.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

claims.summary: Tomato products and citrus or acidic drinks are common individual reflux triggers, especially when the acidic component is prominent.
claims.applicability: Use for tomato sauce, citrus juice, or another clearly acidic food at a meaningful amount, not a trace garnish by itself.
claims.limitations: Food triggers vary substantially, and broad elimination is not supported for every person with GERD.

claims.summary: Spicy foods are a common symptom trigger for some people with GERD.
claims.applicability: Use when meaningful spicy heat is explicitly present. A trace pepper garnish alone should not imply high concern.
claims.limitations: This is an individual trigger pattern, not a universal response, and the supporting evidence is limited.

claims.summary: Coffee and caffeinated drinks are common reflux triggers for some people.
claims.applicability: Use when coffee, espresso, an energy drink, or another clearly caffeinated beverage is present.
claims.limitations: Tolerance is individual. Do not infer caffeine from an unrelated dark beverage or food name.

claims.summary: Alcoholic beverages are common reflux triggers for some people.
claims.applicability: Use only when alcohol is explicitly present, with dose reflected by the serving rather than the number of supporting documents.
claims.limitations: Individual response varies, and trigger avoidance is a conditional recommendation.

claims.summary: Chocolate and peppermint are common reflux triggers for some people.
claims.applicability: Use when chocolate, cocoa, peppermint, or mint is a meaningful component rather than a speculative ingredient.
claims.limitations: These are not universal triggers and should not outweigh direct food facts or personal evidence.

claims.summary: Meal size and timing can affect reflux symptoms, so a clearly large meal can justify more caution than the same food in a small portion.
claims.applicability: Use only when the meal or serving is clearly large or dominant. Do not infer a large serving from a close camera crop.
claims.limitations: Timing is not visible in a scan and should not be invented. For general discomfort this is contextual rather than diagnostic evidence.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease

claims.summary: Carbonated drinks may reproduce reflux symptoms for some people and are distinct from chemical leaveners used in food.
claims.applicability: Use for an explicitly carbonated beverage such as soda, sparkling water, or beer. Baking soda is not a carbonated beverage exposure.
claims.limitations: Individual tolerance varies and carbonation alone should not automatically produce a high concern band.

claims.summary: A limited trial of a low-FODMAP diet can improve global IBS symptoms for some patients, followed by reintroduction and personalization.
claims.applicability: Use as mechanism-level context for meaningful FODMAP exposures, with dose and the person's own history affecting concern.
claims.limitations: A low-FODMAP diet is not a permanent universal avoidance list. It should not make every listed food high concern.

source.title: ACG Clinical Guideline: Management of Irritable Bowel Syndrome

claims.summary: Diet response in IBS is heterogeneous, and structured reintroduction is used to identify personal triggers while broadening the diet.
claims.applicability: Use to keep generic concern proportional and to allow repeated calm or reactive personal evidence to refine the result.
claims.limitations: Personal observations can be confounded and should require repeated paired evidence before moving a band.

source.title: The role of diet in irritable bowel syndrome

claims.summary: FODMAP concern is dose-dependent, so trace, small, standard, and dominant exposures should not receive the same judgment.
claims.applicability: Use the extracted amount and role. Several foods sharing one mechanism aggregate exposure but do not count as independent mechanisms.
claims.limitations: A photograph provides only an estimate of amount, so confidence should fall when portion or ingredients are unclear.

source.title: The role of diet in irritable bowel syndrome

claims.summary: Lactose intolerance symptoms are tied to the amount of lactose consumed and individual tolerance.
claims.applicability: Use for milk and lactose-containing dairy. A large milk-based serving is more concerning than a trace dairy ingredient.
claims.limitations: Dairy fat and milk allergy are different mechanisms. Do not treat every dairy food as containing the same lactose dose.

source.title: Eating, Diet, & Nutrition for Lactose Intolerance
source.organization: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

claims.summary: Yogurt and hard cheeses such as cheddar or Swiss are lower in lactose and may be easier to tolerate than milk for many people.
claims.applicability: Use when the food form is explicitly yogurt or an aged hard cheese. It lowers lactose exposure relative to milk but does not guarantee tolerance.
claims.limitations: Soft cheese, cream, and cheese sauce should not inherit the hard-cheese reduction without evidence.

source.title: Eating, Diet, & Nutrition for Lactose Intolerance
source.organization: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

claims.summary: Lactose-free and lactose-reduced milk products reduce the lactose exposure that drives lactose intolerance symptoms.
claims.applicability: Use only when lactose-free or lactose-reduced status is explicit in text, a product record, or a readable label.
claims.limitations: A photograph of an unlabeled white liquid cannot establish lactose-free status.

source.title: Eating, Diet, & Nutrition for Lactose Intolerance
source.organization: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

claims.summary: Non-celiac wheat sensitivity can involve reproducible symptoms after wheat exposure, but the responsible component may be gluten, fructans, or another wheat component.
claims.applicability: Use the user's selected gluten-sensitivity lens without treating it as celiac disease or assuming that gluten is the only possible trigger.
claims.limitations: The app cannot diagnose non-celiac wheat sensitivity, celiac disease, or wheat allergy from a scan, and expectancy can affect reported symptoms.

source.title: European Society for the Study of Coeliac Disease 2025 Updated Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Management of Coeliac Disease in Adults. Part 1: Diagnostic Approach
source.organization: European Society for the Study of Coeliac Disease

claims.summary: An explicit wheat exposure is relevant for a person who reports reproducible non-celiac wheat or gluten sensitivity symptoms.
claims.applicability: Use when wheat or another gluten-containing grain is explicit or strongly defining. Keep gluten and wheat-fructan mechanisms distinct.
claims.limitations: A scan cannot identify which wheat component causes symptoms, confirm a diagnosis, or establish cross-contact risk from an unlabeled meal.

source.title: European Society for the Study of Coeliac Disease 2025 Updated Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Management of Coeliac Disease in Adults. Part 1: Diagnostic Approach
source.organization: European Society for the Study of Coeliac Disease

claims.summary: In a randomized crossover trial of people with self-reported non-celiac gluten sensitivity, fructans produced more gastrointestinal symptoms than gluten.
claims.applicability: Use to keep wheat-fructan and gluten mechanisms distinct and to avoid claiming that every wheat-related symptom is caused by gluten.
claims.limitations: This trial does not prove that gluten is irrelevant for every individual and should not override clear personal evidence.

source.title: Fructan, Rather Than Gluten, Induces Symptoms in Patients With Self-Reported Non-Celiac Gluten Sensitivity

claims.summary: An unknown sauce or incomplete ingredient list should lower confidence, not automatically imply a high-concern trigger load.
claims.applicability: Use when the food is genuinely unresolved after extraction. Ask for details or suggest a simpler preparation rather than inventing ingredients.
claims.limitations: This is a conservative product rule derived from evidence uncertainty, not a clinical claim about the food.

source.title: The role of diet in irritable bowel syndrome

claims.summary: A clearly rich or fried meal can be harder to tolerate for some people with nonspecific digestive symptoms.
claims.applicability: Use as a cautious general lens only when the fat or fried preparation is meaningful and visible or explicit.
claims.limitations: Nonspecific symptoms have many causes. This supports modest caution, not diagnosis or an automatic high score.

source.title: The role of diet in irritable bowel syndrome
